notes: Traffic fell to 13 transits as the conflict tightened access.
Reports indicated only a single oil tanker was visible that day.
Transit shifted toward selective and controlled passage rather than open commercial flow.
Only four vessels transited the corridor.
The Strait remained largely closed and traffic stayed far below normal commercial levels.
War-risk insurance withdrawals and conflict disruption reinforced selective passage rather than open flow.
Windward reported the Strait remained largely closed on March 3, with only four vessels transiting the corridor.
Traffic stayed dramatically below the recent seven-day average and normal commercial levels.
Conflict disruption and insurance withdrawal reinforced a selective, risk-driven transit environment.
Search-grounded summaries point to only a single ship making the passage around March 5.
A Marshall Islands-flagged cargo ship (Iron Maiden) was cited as one attempted transit during the period.
Commercial traffic was effectively at zero or near-zero as operators delayed passage.
Reporting around March 6 described near-standstill conditions with only a single commercial transit visible around the day.
No oil tankers were highlighted in the minimal daily flow.
The backlog outside the Strait continued to build as operators delayed passage.
Traffic fell to its lowest level since the conflict began, with only two outbound Iranian-flagged vessels and no inbound crossings.
Foreign commercial operators appeared to have effectively withdrawn from the waterway.
At least one tanker may have completed a dark transit with AIS disabled before reappearing later.
Commercial activity reached a new low with just one outbound Iranian-flagged vessel and no inbound movements.
This represented a 50% decrease from the previous day.
Overall flow remained far below normal operating levels.
Windward reported two outbound crossings and no inbound movements.
The vessels included one crude oil tanker and one vessel of unknown classification.
Transit stayed severely constrained and broadly aligned with the depressed 7-day average.
One inbound and two outbound crossings were recorded.
Selective transit allowances appeared to be in effect for a narrow subset of vessels.
Traffic remained far below the 7-day average and near a standstill.
No AIS-confirmed crossings were recorded in either direction.
This marked the first full visible halt in commercial shipping through the chokepoint.
Roughly 400 vessels were still operating in the Gulf of Oman waiting near the Strait.
Three outbound crossings were recorded and no inbound transits.
This was the first visible movement since March 13 but still near paralysis.
Traffic remained only marginally above the 7-day moving average.
One inbound and one outbound vessel were recorded.
Both identified vessels were bulk carriers flagged to Panama.
At least five bulk carriers were observed routing via Iranian territorial waters.
Five AIS-transmitting vessels transited the Strait.
All followed a northern routing pattern above Larak Island.
Traffic remained permission-based and embedded in a worsening mine-risk environment.
Only four AIS-visible crossings were recorded.
One vessel exited the Gulf and three entered via the managed northern corridor.
Standard commercial lanes remained empty while vessels staged north of Larak Island.
Live regional tracking indicated roughly three transits over the prior 24 hours.
Throughput was described as around 5% of a normal 60-vessel day.
Selective passage rather than open commercial navigation remained the operating model.
